questions.question: Why does a chat system need persistent WebSocket connections rather than polling?
options: WebSockets are easier to code
Instant delivery requires the server to PUSH a message the moment it arrives; polling can't do that efficiently and wastes resources at scale
Polling can't carry text
WebSockets use less memory than any alternative
questions.explanation: Chat must feel instant, which means the server pushes to the recipient immediately. Polling adds latency and, at millions of users, generates enormous wasteful traffic. A held-open WebSocket lets the server push the instant a message arrives (Phase 1).

questions.question: Alice (on gateway server 3) sends a message to Bob (on gateway server 47). What's the core routing challenge?
options: Bob's message is too large
Server 3 must find which server holds Bob's live connection and deliver to it — connections are spread across many machines
WebSockets can't cross servers at all
There is no challenge; one server holds everyone
questions.explanation: With millions of connections spread over thousands of gateways, the recipient's socket is likely on a different server. You can't deliver by writing to a database — you need to locate Bob's gateway (a connection registry) and route the message to it (pub/sub).

questions.question: What is the connection registry's role?
options: It stores all message history
It maps each user to the gateway server holding their connection, so messages can be routed to the right server
It rate-limits messages
It encrypts WebSocket traffic
questions.explanation: The registry (in a fast shared store like Redis) records user_id → gateway_server so a sender's gateway can find where the recipient is connected and forward the message (via pub/sub) to that gateway, which pushes it down the recipient's socket.

questions.question: Why should a chat system persist a message BEFORE attempting delivery?
options: To make delivery faster
So a crash mid-delivery, or an offline recipient, never causes message loss — durability first, then deliver
Because databases require it
To avoid using WebSockets
questions.explanation: If you push first and store later, a crash during delivery loses the message. Persisting first means an offline user gets it on reconnect and crashes don't drop messages — trading a tiny bit of latency for the reliable delivery the requirements demand.

questions.question: How is correct per-conversation message ordering achieved?
options: By requiring global ordering across all conversations
Using a time-sortable message ID (e.g. Snowflake) and partitioning the message store by conversation_id, so each conversation's messages store and read in order
By sending messages alphabetically
Ordering is impossible in distributed chat
questions.explanation: Users only need messages within a conversation to be ordered, not a global order across all chats. A time-sortable ID plus partitioning by conversation_id gives each conversation a consistent, time-ordered sequence — cheaper than global ordering (Phase 5).
